data.title: Permianville Royalty Trust: Do Not Expect A Distribution Anytime Soon
data.description:
About two years ago, I analyzed the risks of Permianville Royalty Trust (NYSE:PVL), namely its proven vulnerability to downtrends in the prices of oil and gas and its poor record of distributions, which have been suspended more often than those of other oil and gas trusts, such as Sabine Royalty Trust (SBR) and Cross Timbers Royalty Trust (CRT). Since that article, the stock has vastly underperformed the S&P 500 and the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLE), as the stock has offered a total return of -31% whereas the S&P 500 has rallied 36% and the ETF of the energy sector has remained essentially flat (including its dividends).

I reiterated the risks of Permianville Royalty Trust almost three months after the first article. Since that article, the trust has underperformed the S&P 500 and the ETF of the energy sector in a similar fashion. Moreover, the trust has suspended its distributions since December due to elevated operating and development expenses, which have exceeded revenues. Investors should not be misled by the distribution of $0.0085 (0.6%) that was announced last month, as this distribution has resulted from an asset sale and hence it is special, i.e., non-recurring.

Going forward, the outlook of the global oil market has significantly deteriorated. The trust suspended its distributions in December, when the price of oil was much higher. Given also a weak outlook for the global oil market, investors should not expect Permianville Royalty Trust to resume its distributions anytime soon.

Business overview

Permianville Royalty Trust has a 14-year history and is based in Houston. It receives 80% of the net profits from the sale of oil and natural gas output from properties in Texas, Louisiana, and New Mexico. In 2024, the trust generated 82% of its revenues from oil and the remaining 18% from natural gas. Therefore, this is primarily an oil trust and hence investors should carefully examine the outlook of the oil market before making any investment decisions on the stock.

The average realized price of oil of the trust remained essentially flat at $79 in 2024 vs. 2023 whereas the average realized price of natural gas plunged 46%. Given also a 64% increase in the costs of the company, primarily due to high development expenses, total distributions slumped 76%, from $0.37 in 2023 to $0.09 in 2024.

Even worse, costs have exceeded revenues since November. As a result, Permianville Royalty Trust has suspended its distributions since December. Moreover, on March 17th, the trust reported that the deficit between costs and revenues decreased from $1.4 million to $1.1 million during the last month. As long as a deficit remains in place, the trust cannot reinstate its distributions.

It is also important to note that the outlook for the price of oil has greatly deteriorated lately. Last month, OPEC announced that it would begin to restore its production to normal levels on April 1st. Indeed, the cartel has begun to boost its output, with a goal to increase it by approximately 2.0 million barrels per day by the end of next year. This is a game changer for the oil price, as OPEC and Russia have implemented deep production cuts over the last three years and thus they have provided a solid floor to the price of oil, around $70.

The other game changer is the financial turmoil that has been caused by the announcement of excessive tariffs by the new U.S. government. The current level of tariffs is the highest since the 1930s. Such high tariffs are likely to hurt global trade and essentially reverse the benefits reaped from the secular trend of globalization, which had prevailed over the last three decades. Due to the excessive tariffs, Goldman Sachs recently raised the probability of an upcoming recession from 20% to 45%.

Even if a recession does not show up, a slowdown of the global economy seems inevitable right now. The Energy Information Administration [EIA] just slashed its forecast for global oil consumption by 490,000 barrels per day for 2025 and by 620,000 barrels per day for 2026. It also cut its forecast for the average price of oil in 2025 from $70.7 to $63.9 and the price of oil in 2026 from $65.0 to $57.5. As the average realized price of oil of Permianville Royalty Trust was $79 in both 2023 and 2024, it is evident that the trust will probably realize much lower oil prices in 2025 and 2026. Consequently, the company is unlikely to resume its distributions anytime soon.

Of course, an unexpected geopolitical event, which could disrupt part of global oil supply and trigger a rally of oil prices, could occur at some point in the future. There is still an ongoing war in Ukraine, while there is tension in the Middle East. However, it has proved virtually impossible to predict such events and hence it is prudent for investors not to base their investing thesis on such incidents.

Valuation

Permianville Royalty Trust has not posted profits and has not offered distributions so far this year, except for the recent special distribution that resulted from an asset sale. Consequently, the stock cannot be evaluated based on the usual metrics, namely a P/E ratio or a comparison between its current distribution yield and its historical average distribution yield.

On the other hand, the trust has offered an average annual distribution per unit of $0.23 over the last five years and $0.27 over the last decade. If the trust manages to offer an annual distribution equal to its 5-year average of $0.23 in the future, it will be offering a 16.4% yield at its current stock price of $1.40. Of course, the trust has a long way to go to return to its historical average distribution. In other words, it will need a strong recovery of the price of oil, above $75. Given the aforementioned headwinds facing the global oil market, there are absolutely no signs on the horizon of such a strong recovery of the price of oil.

Proved reserves

The total proved reserves of Permianville Royalty Trust have decreased by 5% in total over the last two years, from 5.392 to 5.096 million barrels of oil equivalent. With the use of a model based on discounted future cash flows, the trust estimates that its discounted future cash flows amount to $91.362 million or $2.77 per unit. The trust has used an average oil price of $75.48 to estimate its reserves and has used an annual discount rate of 10% to reflect the estimated time of future cash flows. The oil price used in the estimate is the oil price at the end of 2024.

It is essentially impossible to forecast future oil prices with any degree of accuracy due to their high sensitivity to a series of unpredictable factors, such as global economic growth, growth in the use of clean energy sources and geopolitical events. However, the trust has estimated its future cash flows in a well-established way, in the best way it can do so based on the current available data.

On the one hand, the present value of future cash flows of $2.77 per unit is nearly twice as much as the current stock price of the trust. As a result, one could conclude that the stock is undervalued. On the other hand, future oil prices, which are unpredictable, will greatly affect the actual cash flows of the trust in the future. It is also important to note that the estimate of the trust was determined before the recent plunge of the price of oil. Overall, given the gloomy outlook of the global oil market, the proven vulnerability of Permianville Royalty Trust to downturns of the oil market and the suspension of distributions in recent months, it is prudent to have a wider margin of safety than the current one before buying the stock.

Cash position

Permianville Royalty Trust has no net debt; instead it has a net cash position (cash – total liabilities) of $2.0 million. The net cash position has remained between $1.0 and $2.0 million in each of the last 8 quarters. The trust has incurred high development expenses in recent months, but it is not likely to face any liquidity issues, as these expenses are likely to normalize at some point. Overall, investors should not worry about the need for additional financing. If challenging conditions remain in place for long, they will extend the existence of the current deficit and hence they will delay the reinstatement of distributions.

Final thoughts

Permianville Royalty Trust is currently facing a perfect storm, namely high operating costs, which exceed revenues, a boost of production by OPEC and the financial turmoil caused by the excessive tariffs of the U.S. As the trust suspended its distributions at much higher oil prices, it is not likely to resume its distributions anytime soon. Nevertheless, whenever the price of oil recovers strongly, probably in a few years, the trust is likely to return towards its historical average distributions and reward its unitholders. Overall, the stock receives a hold rating but only for patient investors, who can maintain a long-term perspective during the ongoing downturn.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Intel: Ignore The Tariffs, Buy The Turnaround
data.description:
I recently published a piece on Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) on January 21, 2025, suggesting it was a buy at $22/share (since then it has fallen by 10.39%) on par with the S&P 500 (down 10.8% during the same period). In this article, I reiterate my buy opinion on INTC despite the sharp decline in broader market indices in the past few weeks.

I believe INTC is still a buy despite recent market turbulence and tariff concerns, which are more than positively offset by bullish calaysts such as the recently reported discussions of a potential JV with Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM). My bullish reasons are below:

1. Tariffs will have minimal impact on INTC

The tariff rollercoaster in the past few weeks has been well documented (for example, this live update by BBC). Currently, the market is mainly worried about the negative impact on INTC of the tit-for-tat trade tariff escalation between the US and China. Personally, I think these worries are overblown for INTC: While the US and China may move to diversify supply chains for goods that are easily substitutable, INTC is supplying products that China cannot produce on its own yet and one way or another China will continue to buy these products (either directly or indirectly). Given the on-again/off-again tariff escalations and de-escalations, I think it will eventually become a lesser factor while the bullish factors below become more predominant.

Latest news over the weekend indicates the US will exempt chips from reciprocal tariffs, and China will probably reciprocate in kind given it still needs INTC products, hence tariff impact on INTC will probably be minimal.

2. INTC will benefit from the JV with TSMC if successfully established

INTC’s stock has not done well in the past decade or even two decades.

But beneath the lack of progress in its stock price, INTC has a strong core business (CPUs) and capabilities (semiconductor foundry) that few other companies have in the USA. Given a conducive environment for success (a second chance and good leadership), I think INTC has a fair chance of a turnaround.

If we look at the example of Chrysler bailout in 1979, even though the bailout was widely debated at the time, with competent management (and given the concentrated market share of the auto market), Chrysler was able to turnaround in the 1980s and the stock became one of Peter Lynch’s most storied investments).

Similarly, I believe INTC is undervalued due a long period of lackluster performance and its market cap doesn’t reflect its actual potential. But recently things have been moving fast: INTC got a new CEO (more on that below) and there is persistent news about INTC and TSMC forming some sort of JV:

  • It appears this JV idea is progressing - rumors of a similar arrangement were first floated in February 2025;

  • This week, latest news reports suggest discussions are still undergoing but sounds like there are more details than in February (TSMC would form a JV with INTC and receive 20% of the shares, in exchange for operating INTC’s US fabs). The Reuters report suggest that the US government (through the Commerce department etc) are involved in the deal-making..

My assessment is that the JV with TSMC will probably entail some form of know-how transfer from TSMC to help INTC’s foundry business regain its footing and competitiveness. And what does TSMC get out of it? This would bring me to my next point, namely INTC is set to benefit from the broader economic nationalism tailwind.

3. Intel benefits from economic nationalism trends in the USA

Economic nationalism (which I would loosely define as being focused on keeping economic activities within a country’s borders, as opposed to globalization where production, R&D, consumption etc may occur in vastly different geographies) appears to be more prominent these days ( I’ll let the pundits debate whether we are undergoing globalization or de-globalization and the merits and demerits of it all) but there’s no denying it is becoming more evident:

  • Witness the case of Japan’s Nippon Steel’s turbulent and so far unsuccessful acquisition of US Steel. Part of the reason for blocking the acquisition is to prevent an important industrial sector from falling under foreign control. Though the Trump administration is still opposed to the merger, it appears they still want Nippon Steel to invest in US Steel, which looks very similar to the deal being rumored between INTC and TSM.

  • The recent market turbulence from broad tariffs levied by the US on imports also reflects similar economic nationalism trends.

  • Under economic nationalist thinking, market access is valuable in and of itself and should not be taken for granted. This was actually the norm historically - in the past 500 years since the Age of Discovery, European nations often concentrated trade within their alliance systems and periods of “globalization” where goods traded without borders were a fluke rather than the norm. (This is a recurring theme in Peter Zeihan’s books which are thought-provoking even if some readers point out details they don’t agree with or believe to be inaccurate.)

This overall background has two bullish implications for INTC:

  • Given INTC is the only company in the USA with any substantial foundry capacities (and given how expensive it is to build these facilities and maintain the manpower capabilities), it makes sense for the USA to support INTC to become competitive and self-sustaining.

  • The USA has strong leverage over TSMC (market access, tariffs and otherwise) to induce it to support INTC to bring it up to speed. The specifics of any potential deal are not public yet so this is my conjecture, but it seems not too far-fetched, given the current US administration’s focus and highlighting of over $3 trillion in investment pledges from companies as part of their economic agenda.

4. INTC new CEO’s incentives are highly aligned with shareholders

INTC’s stock price spiked initially on news of the new CEO Lip-bu Tan taking the reins. Tan’s pay package highly aligns his interests with shareholders:

  • Base salary of $1 million and performance bonus up to 200% of base salary.

  • $66 million of potential value in long-term equity awards and stock options. The value and vesting of these will mainly be tied to the total shareholder return of INTC shares (as well as in comparison against overall S&P 500 performance).

  • Moreover, Tan has to buy $25 million in INTC shares as part of his employment deal. Instead of stock options which are to some extent “playing with the house’s (or in this case, the shareholder’s) money”, essentially Tan is ponying up real cash to buy shares with his own money in order just to get the CEO job, so he has a lot of skin in the game to make this work. If INTC’s stock is down more than 20% after 5 years, Tan might end up with no or negative income (as an illustrative example before considering taxes, $5 million base salary income netted off against 20*$25=$5 million loss in stock) from his tenure as CEO.

5. Intel’s valuation is rock bottom

INTC currently has a market cap of $86 billion. As I evaluated in my previous article, INTC’s CPU business alone has “core” after-tax net income potential of $12 billion a year based on (i) its performance from FY19 to FY21 and (ii) the market dynamics of the CPU market (with stagnant and even declining PC sales and market interest focused on AI chips, I believe competition in CPUs is likely to be favorable for INTC to maintain existing market share and profitability). Such a level of after-tax net income would correspond to a P/E of 7, with everything else (including foundry etc) all for free.

While the foundry (note: this is also known as fab) business may seem like a drag right now (for example, this article quotes a Citi research report that questions if INTC’s foundry business can ever get back on track), I think the market is way underpricing the potential of INTC’s foundry business:

  • If it is brought back to life under good management and cooperation with TSMC, the foundry business may become another glowing bright spot in INTC’s product portfolio as the market realizes it is the only noteworthy fab in the USA with largely American ownership and history (while TSMC has built and is building some fabs in the US, it is still somewhat of a foreign company).

  • Tech companies throughout the ecosystem are rooting for INTC as an alternative to TSMC. INTC doesn’t even need to be as good as TSMC, as long as its products provide acceptable quality for the price charged, even if still lagging behind TSMC, tech companies will still buy from INTC in order to diversify their supply chain. TSMC needs to get an A but INTC can achieve a C and still scrape by. And if INTC achieves a B or even A-, then its foundry business will be vastly outperforming expectations, which, I believe, will then flow through into the financials and stock price.

Risks to bullish thesis

Now there are some obvious risks with the bullish thesis:

  1. A lot of the rumors so far have just been rumors and news reports, from acquisitions of INTC to potential deals/JV between INTC and some other companies. It is still possible that none of this comes true, or even if it comes true, the intended results are not achieved (e.g. INTC does not gain much of value from its JV with TSMC and the JV slowly withers on the vine).

  2. It is also possible that even if the US government etc does all this for INTC, INTC still squanders the chance to regain its footing. Nothing’s guaranteed to succeed, and the chip business is both cyclical and ferociously competitive.

  3. Given what’s going on with the broader tech industry and economy, short-term downturns/recessions could negatively impact INTC far more in the short run, and any long-term boost will take some time before it's evident (if ever).

  4. It's also possible that the US and China's tariff spat continues for an extended period and hurts INTC's sales.

These are all valid concerns but with a broad array of stakeholders such as tech industry ecosystem and the US government rooting for INTC, I believe if something (e.g. INTC’s foundry business) is valued at nearly 0 and the odds are stacked in its favor, the risk-reward is very high. This may be a “Chrysler moment” for INTC and years later if it turns around, investors may wonder why they were focused on the $2.3 trillion market cap Nvidia (NVDA) while ignoring the diamond in the rough INTC.

Conclusion

As per my previous article, I am long INTC with a very small portion of my portfolio as a tactical/speculative buy. I consider current prices attractive and would buy more if it dips, though turnaround situations are tricky, and I would put a very limited proportion of my portfolio in INTC (no more than a few % if even that). I think INTC is attractive in terms of risk/reward and with these persistent tailwinds.

Whether tech stocks or the overall market goes up and down, the current market cap for INTC is likely already reflective of a lot of the bad news and poor expectations, so I believe even in the worst-case scenario, INTC is unlikely to suffer too big a permanent loss in value. Put it this way, even if the current tech sell-off continues, NVDA may lose a significant portion of its $2.3 trillion market cap and not recover for a long time (if ever) even in the next bull market, while any decline in INTC’s $86 billion market cap will probably be regained in the next bull market.

data.title: NetApp: Disappointing Guidance Paved The Way For An Attractive Price In This Strong Company
data.description:
Introduction

NetApp, Inc.(NASDAQ:NTAP) is one of the leading storage infrastructure providers in the world. Recently, NetApp's stock price has plunged due to underwhelming guidance, which disappointed Wall Street. The decline was further exacerbated by President Trump's Liberation Day, which saw its stock price drop to almost $70 per share. The stock recovered after President Trump paused the reciprocal tariffs for 90 days, and the stock price is now hovering at around $83 per share, which, I believe, is still a good price to pay for the company. Despite these setbacks, NetApp's fundamentals remain strong, and more importantly, the company now sells at a more attractive level that, I believe, is fair, considering its historical lack of growth. In my first article about NetApp, I called a 'Hold' rating for this stock as I believe that considering the company's low historical growth rate doesn't justify the high multiple the company sells at, even though AI can propel the industry's growth. However, the stock is now selling at a more reasonable free cash flow yield of around 9%. Thus, I will upgrade this stock to a 'Buy' rating.

Q3 2025 Earnings Takeaway

Consolidated Financial Performance

On February 27, 2025, NetApp reported its Q3 2025 earnings call. For the quarter, NetApp generated revenue of $1.64B, an increase of 2.2% YoY, on the lower end of their Q3 guidance of $1.61B to $1.76B.

Management attributed the top-line underperformance to poor sales execution, which pushed some deals to the next quarter, and unfavourable FOREX rates. Gross margin declined by 2% due to the higher cost associated with the SSD pre-buy the management did earlier this calendar year. Turning to NetApp's operating margin, the company generated a non-GAAP operating profit of around $492M, an increase of 1.4% YoY and a 30% operating margin above mid-Q3 guidance. Note that NetApp's non-GAAP operating income includes shared-based compensations, restructuring charges, and amortization of intangible assets, which management believes best represents the company's financial performance. Deducting these expenses, NetApp's operating income would be $362m, translating to a 22% GAAP operating margin.

Segmental Performance

Turning over to NetApp’s performance in the All-Flash Array segment, a key growth area for the company. All Flash Array for Q3 2025 now has a revenue run rate of $3.8B, an increase of 10% YoY. All-Flash Array now makes up 58% of NetApp’s total revenue, which management intends to increase over time as it is one of the drivers for their gross margin expansion.

NetApp's all-flash array business performance is crucial, as proven by Pure Storage's (PSTG) superb growth and market share gains by specializing in it. Despite being a relatively young enterprise founded in 2009, Pure Storage has outperformed decade-long storage incumbents like Dell EMC (DELL), NetApp, HPE (HPE), and IBM (IBM), among others, in an industry that remained stagnant.

It is safe to assume that this segment is where most of NetApp's growth will come from, especially with AI preferring performant storage systems, as evidenced by all the recent certifications that storage enterprise companies did with Nvidia.

Turning to public cloud performance, this segment is NetApp's bet that the future data storage market will be hybrid, with some data on the cloud and some on-premise. The public cloud generated revenue of $174M and a gross margin of an impressive 76.4%.

The public cloud is another promising business for NetApp. It is an accretive gross margin opportunity for the company, as its gross margin already surpasses its consolidated gross margin. It also leverages NetApp's foundational proprietary technology, ONTAP. This means that it most likely takes advantage of the fixed cost associated with R&D, meaning the company doesn't need more overhead to offer this feature to customers; in short, it can be accretive to the operating margin as more revenue comes in.

Another notable update in the earnings call is NetApp’s divestiture of Spot and CloudChekr, both of which provide automation tools for cloud operation, to Flexera for $100M, a considerable drop from the acquisition price of Spot, valued at $450M. Despite the significant loss from the transaction, this is the right move by NetApp as it allows the company to focus fully on its core business. A management’s time and attention are scarce resources for any company. Thus, it makes sense to put these scarce resources on a company’s core business or what made it successful in the first place, in NetApp’s case, the enterprise storage business.

I believe this quarter is a good one for NetApp and value investors. The company continues to deliver superb gross margin, operating margin, and return on capital. Wall Street's disappointment with NetApp's guidance paved the way for investors, especially value ones, looking to invest in a strong company that can benefit from AI. While the growth is subpar so far, the enterprise storage market will eventually benefit from the increasing adoption rate of AI among enterprises; NetApp stands to benefit whether enterprise AI is running on the cloud or on-premise with its unified data approach.

Competitive Equilibrium

The enterprise storage market is dominated by multiple players, with Dell EMC leading with an almost 30% market share, and can be labeled as 'fragmented.' However, despite the presence of multiple players, the enterprise storage industry has been very stable, reflected in the market share movement of what, I believe, are the best companies in this space since 2020.

Of course, there are some small market share losses and gains occasionally; however, the non-volatile movement of the participants' market share indicates that the industry has a competitive advantage or moat that makes it hard for new entrants to enter and gain market share easily. Pure Storage is the only recent entrant that has experienced success in the enterprise storage market. This can be attributed to the high cost of switching data from one storage vendor to another. This makes trust and reliability or 'guarantee' the most crucial value proposition in this industry, which is hard to gain from a strong incumbent.

Why is it a stable industry, despite the presence of multiple players, still good to invest in? Because all participants in a stable industry behave according to what's best for the industry: maximizing profits and returns exceeding the cost of capital. This is evident in NetApp's return on capital employed, which is clearly above the cost of capital. Since the balance sheets of other industry participants are consolidated into other parts of their business, we can use NetApp to get a clear picture of the industry's superb return on capital.

Of course, there will be some discrepancies with other companies as they compete on different server verticals, which can result in loss of focus and underperformance; NetApp's focus on enterprise storage may have contributed to its returns. However, factors such as high switching cost and the similarity of the features, innovation, and products make me lean towards the industry equilibrium as the major attribute that makes NetApp's financial return possible. Furthermore, they have done this despite the entry of an aggressive competitor in the form of Pure Storage.

While there can be some competitive moves from time to time that aim to take advantage of an opponent's mistakes, resulting in share gain and loss among the participants, it usually doesn't result in the degradation of the industry, resulting in cutthroat competition and price wars that diminish the industry participant's profitability and competing away the cost of capital exceeding returns back to a minimum just enough to satisfy or attract investors' capital. For example, an industry that returns a 30% return on capital will attract entrants eager to grab a slice of the good pie, resulting in a price war that destroys the industry's profitability and eliminates that excess return on capital.

With the entry of disruptive or transformational innovation in the form of AI in the enterprise storage industry, will it be a disruption for the enterprise storage market that will result in drastic share gains for some participants, resulting in the disturbance of the industry's equilibrium? I don't think so. Why? The simplest explanation is that Nvidia, the clear leader in AI, is not backward-integrating into the storage. Instead, they collaborate with the existing enterprise storage solution providers, certifying their storage offerings into Nvidia's DGX AI platform. In short, whatever the industry offers now is good enough for Nvidia's vision in AI. The current way of doing things in the enterprise storage industry will likely continue as the industry's offerings satisfy what the AI industry needs. The value proposition that enterprise decision-makers consider when choosing a data storage solution, such as security and guarantees, will remain the same. Therefore, the existing players that dominate the industry will likely continue to do so in the future.

Valuation

In my first article, I used discounted cash flow to determine NetApp's intrinsic value, which, I believe, is the most appropriate method due to the industry's predictability, high switching cost, and stability. I concluded that despite the company's strong Moat, I believe it should sell lower due to the low growth of the enterprise storage industry despite how AI can potentially propel the industry into a new growth phase. I called a 'Hold' rating as you never want to sell a strong business unless the market offers you a premium that you think is absurd when considering the underlying fundamentals of a company; at the time, NetApp was selling at what, I believe, is a fairly valued price. While storage will benefit from the continued adoption of AI among enterprises, it will take time, as hurdles still remain that prevent enterprises from realizing value on their AI investments. With the stock now declining to a more attractive price of $83 per share, using last year's free cash flow of around $1.53B translates to around 9% free cash flow yield($16.8B current market cap / $1.53B free cash flow). 9% yield for a strong company with a favorable tailwind from AI? I say that is a good deal. Thus, I will upgrade this stock to a 'Buy' rating.

Risk

As I laid out in my first article and this article, I believe that high switching costs and competitive equilibrium will continue to put NetApp in a position of profitable growth. However, as always, the future is uncertain. There is no way of knowing whether the status quo will continue. An entrant or disruptive innovation could upend the equilibrium and switch the profit pool enjoyed by companies such as NetApp away from enterprise storage and into other parts of the storage verticals. However, the past always provides a rough guide to the future. Based on available facts, I believe that the current market condition that allows NetApp to excel will remain the same. Even with the advent of AI, the status quo will likely continue, as whatever the enterprise storage currently offers is good enough for what AI needs.

data.title: Amentum: Stock Down, Setup Improving, Growth Potential
data.description:
I started covering defense and energy contractor Amentum Holdings, Inc. (NYSE:AMTM) in February. The stock was under pressure from ownership issues that many spin-offs face and the question of DOGE and how aggressively they might cut US government spending, which accounts for around 80% of Amentum’s revenue. This brought down not only Amentum but almost all the defense contractors.

My assessment concluded that Amentum’s exposure to DOGE cuts was low because it is concentrated in servicing (e.g., looking after fleets of aircraft) or in primary research (e.g. development of new materials for spacecraft) not in running the overdue, over-budget and antiquated weapons programs that DOGE seems to be targeting. Broadly, I concluded that Amentum’s business aligned well with the priorities of the Trump administration. For the full analysis, take a look at my previous article.

In the intervening two months, the stock has performed poorly, though almost exactly in line with the S&P 500.

Meanwhile, we are closer to getting to the end of the spin-off overhangs, and DOGE risk is receding somewhat. Going forward, Amentum should be a stable business growing revenue at mid-single digits with roughly 10% earnings growth for the next few years, as per management’s guidance. Now trading at an 8.5x forward PE multiple, I think a fair multiple for Amentum would be around 15x, leading to near double from here. Accordingly, I rate the shares a strong buy.

Spin-Off Overhangs Receding

Well-understood factors that often negatively affect spin-offs in early trading including a lack of Wall Street coverage, forced selling from institutions, selling from retail investors in the RemainCo who were given shares and the RemainCo selling off its stake. Amentum has suffered from all of these. Since my last article, there have been a few developments on these fronts.

Wall Street Coverage

When I wrote my first article, there were three analysts covering the stock. Better than none, but significantly less than competitors with similar revenue and market cap. Now The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS) and Cantor Fitzgerald have initiated coverage, at both Neutral/Hold. This is not in itself a catalyst for the stock, it’s just an underlying factor that raises awareness and makes heavy institutional buying more likely.

An important factor to note is that despite these hold ratings, the earnings estimates from the two new analysts have not changed the Wall Street average for FY2025 and FY2026. In fact, they have increased slightly: $2.12 for 2025 vs $2.11, $2.34 for 2026 vs $2.31. Also, both their targets are above the current stock price.

Jacobs’ Divestiture

Amentum was previously a private equity owned company which was combined with a division of Jacobs Solutions Inc. (J) in the spin-off. At the time of the spin, Jacobs owned 7.5% of the shares outstanding, but indicated its intention to sell these within a year. Recently, Jacobs completed this part of the transaction, which means it has no more shares to sell. However, Jacobs could still end up with a further 9,732,087 Amentum shares which are currently held in escrow “subject to final determination of certain performance milestones in connection with a post-closing adjustment to the merger consideration”. Jacobs has said that if the performance obligations are met, and it receives the shares, they will be distributed to current Jacobs shareholders. This might result in more liquidation, but the shares amount to 3% of the shares outstanding, so the effect should be marginal.

It is often said that in a bear market, stocks return to their rightful owners. We have seen a bit of that happening in the last few weeks. When companies are spun off, it often takes them a while to find their rightful owners. Over the first few months, those that cannot, or don’t want to own the stock sell out. Meanwhile, as awareness of the company increases, it starts to attract a more committed investor base. The confluence of these two factors has created substantial selling pressure in the last few weeks.

Business Prospects Improving

In principle, Amentum’s business should be very stable and defensive. The company describes itself as follows: “Amentum Holdings Inc. provides engineering and technology solutions to the U.S. and allied government agencies. It offers supporting programs of critical national importance across energy and environmental, intelligence, space, defense, civilian, and commercial end-markets.” Thus, it operates with long-duration contracts from the most credit-worthy institutions on the planet. For an in-depth look at the business, I refer you to my previous article. The slide below shows how the company operates across diverse end markets:

However, this reliance on the US government for 80% of revenue, which would have previously been seen as a strength, became seen as a weakness with the creation of DOGE and the commotion it caused in the first weeks of the new administration. This was compounded with new Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth’s suggestion that he could cut the Pentagon’s budget by 40% over the Presidency.

I argued that Amentum would not suffer from the DOGE cuts in a meaningful way, given its contracts are based around drone development and defense (which DOGE is keen on), space (which Musk has to rescue himself from given NASA’s contracts with SpaceX), border protection (aligned with the administration) and nuclear energy (which Musk and many DOGE backers support). It is also involved in a lot of service programs, taking care of fleets of military airplanes for example, programs which are much less likely to be cut entirely than overdue and out-of-date weapons development. My conclusion was that while there may be a little churn, most of Amentum’s programs would likely be maintained, and their huge backlog of $45.2bn (more than 3x the FY2024 revenues of the combined company) would eventually fill any gaps that might occur.

Now we have a piece of news which should largely alleviate those DOGE and Pentagon budget fears. On 7 April, it was announced that the President would be seeking a $1tr defense budget, higher than the current budget and higher than all expectations. As is often the case with this administration, this announcement was light on detail. It’s unclear whether this $1tr is just for the Pentagon, in which case it’s a huge boost, or for all Defense agencies, in which case it’s more like a 10% boost. The President commented: “Nobody’s seen anything like it. We have to build our military, and we’re very cost-conscious, but the military is something that we have to build. And we have to be strong because you’ve got a lot of bad forces out there now.”

This change in rhetoric should largely alleviate the fears presented by DOGE and potential Pentagon budget cuts. Although churn is to be expected with the armed services looking to make cuts in their individual budgets and use the proceeds for modernization efforts, and Congress needs to approve the budget, it’s safe to say the worst-case scenario has been taken off the table. Accordingly, I expect Amentum, and indeed other defense contractors, to be accorded higher multiples over the next year or so.

Insider Buying

A key factor to look for with spin-offs is insider buying. Since these stocks are often mispriced initially, insiders often have an opportunity to buy at discounted prices before the market starts to value them properly. I mentioned last time a large purchase of more than $2m by Executive Chair Steven Demetriou, increasing his existing holdings by around 20%. This has been followed by a much smaller purchase (4,600 shares for $97,635) by director Barbara Loughran. Loughran is an accountant by training and was a PwC partner for 20 years. Although this would make her wealthy by almost anyone’s standards, I would guess that $100,000 is not an insignificant amount of money to her. Furthermore, she was recently granted 11,564 shares in the company (in options), meaning she had no obligation to put up her own money to participate in equity upside. Though we can’t be sure of her motivations, there’s a good chance that she sees value in the stock.

Risks

Although some risks have been mitigated by recent events, as with any company, there are still some to watch out for.

The first is the high level of debt that Amentum carries. At last report, it was over $4.6bn, compared to $522m cash on the balance sheet and $223m in trailing twelve month free cash flow. Guidance for this year’s free cash flow is $500m, which provides a little more comfort. Debt repayment is one of the company’s main priorities over the next few years, so investors will want to see them achieve those free cash flow targets and bring down the leverage ratio over time. I think it’s very unlikely that Amentum sees its business become unprofitable on a free cash flow basis, but if for any reason it does start to lose money, the debt load will be a serious concern.

Even with the reduced DOGE risk, it’s still worth watching out for any signs of revoked contracts or a halt in awards. A significant data point here will be the backlog. If we see this deteriorate substantially over the next few quarters, it might be a sign of government austerity. It’s so large that some reduction is to be expected, but it’s more the pace, and management’s commentary, that will be material. Another data point worth keeping an eye on is announced contract wins on Amentum’s website. By my count, between the effective date of the spin-off in September 2024 and when I wrote my article in February of this year, Amentum posted ten press releases announcing contract wins with the US government. In the two months since my article, they haven’t posted any press releases concerning US government work. This might be a sign that plans are on hold. It might also have something to do with the cadence of decision-making in the government, although there were two press releases in March 2024. Until we get confirmation from management that projects are moving forward, or press releases announcing new business, it’s not possible to say DOGE risk is in the rearview mirror.

Valuation

I ventured a valuation for Amentum in my previous article by putting a multiple on FY2026 earnings, assuming the DOGE picture would be clearer by then and that the various spin-off related overhangs would be behind us.

The analyst consensus for FY2026 earnings $2.34.

To determine a reasonable multiple, I looked at historical earnings multiples for similar companies like Leidos Holdings, Inc. (LDOS), CACI International Inc (CACI) and V2X, Inc. (VVX), which came out around 15x. Here is a base bull and bear case based on these assumptions:

Bear

This model is based on a fairly narrow range of estimates, reflecting my belief that Amentum has a stable business which is unlikely to be affected badly by macroeconomic conditions. However, it’s always the case that things could turn out worse than my bear case. I just think that the bear case is the lower bound of the probable outcomes. That means that in all probable outcomes, Amentum looks like a good buy at these levels, and could perhaps be a very good buy with some beats on earnings and a peer multiple.

Conclusion

In the two months since my first article on Amentum, some of the spin-off related overhangs have dissipated and the threat of DOGE cuts has become less severe. Meanwhile, the general market concern over the current trade war (by which Amentum will be less affected than most) has sent the stock down, meaning that it represents better value.

With improving business prospects and a more compelling valuation, I upgrade Amentum to a strong buy.

data.title: 8 U.S. REITs Announce Dividend Increases In March
data.description:
Eight publicly traded real estate investment trusts in the US announced increases to their regular dividends in March, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data.

Hotel REIT Chatham Lodging Trust (CLDT) declared the highest percentage increase in quarterly dividend, raising its payout 28.6% to 9 cents per share. The new dividend, payable to shareholders on April 15, is 72.7% lower than it was before the COVID-19 pandemic.

Healthcare REIT CareTrust REIT Inc. (CTRE) announced the second-highest increase, adjusting its payout by 15.5% to 33.5 cents per share. The increased dividend will be paid to shareholders on or around April 15.

The third-highest increase was announced by shopping center REIT InvenTrust Properties Corp. (IVT), which boosted its quarterly dividend by 5.0% to 23.76 cents per share. The dividend will be paid to shareholders on or around April 15.

Retail REIT Choice Properties REIT (OTC:PPRQF) raised its monthly cash distribution by 1.3% to 6.4167 Canadian cents per trust unit, the highest dividend among Canadian REITs, payable to unitholders April 15.

Dividend hikes by sector

The first quarter of 2025 ended with 36 US REITs, representing 22.8% of the sector, announcing increases to their dividends, according to Market Intelligence data.

Among US specialty REITs, seven companies or 38.9% of the industry declared dividend hikes during the quarter. Among industrial REITs, five companies, or 38.5% of the industry, announced higher cash payouts.

Four Canadian REITs declared dividend hikes during the first quarter, representing 8.7% of all Canada-based REITs.

data.title: Enphase: This Investment Doesn't Come Without Risk
data.description:
Today I want to take a look at Enphase (NASDAQ:ENPH), and understand why I would recommend investors to be wary, even though the solar industry is expected to grow at really high rates through the next 25 years. My most prominent reason for cautiousness is the rising competition from Tesla, which might run the company into the ground. Nevertheless, To understand my BUY rating further, we will first take a look at what is going on with the solar energy market, to then analyze the company's fundamentals using the 9 Pillar Analysis, and finally calculate their intrinsic value through two of our intrinsic valuation models.

Introduction

Enphase is a company that specializes in building solar-powered home energy systems that are easily upgradable, but most importantly smart, and fully controllable via the company's software. The company has seen great growth over the past decade, with their revenue increasing by 280.55% in that time frame. The issue is that recently, they have been facing headwinds such as rising competition, decreasing solar-panel prices and most recently, tariff concerns.

Analysis

Market Overview

Firstly, I want to take a look at the company's manufacturing site locations, as that is one of the main reasons to why it is vulnerable to potential tariffs. Enphase currently has 5 manufacturing sites, of which only two are located in the US. The others are located in India, China and Mexico. Furthermore, both of the company's only cell pack suppliers are located in China, which faces the highest threat from tariffs. If the tariffs were to go through, taking into account that in 2024, 70.23% of the company's revenue came from the US, the company would face great declines in their profitability.

Looking to the second issue before moving to the positives, the danger from competition. Tesla, even though being an EV manufacturer, has been generating a decent part of their revenue through their Powerwall segment, which essentially offers the same product and service as Enphase. In 2024, Tesla's revenue from their energy segment stood at $10.09B, accounting for a YOY growth of 67.00%.

When we compare this to Enphase's YOY revenue decrease of 41.95%, things do seem to start looking a little dim. Although it can be argued that Enphase's product, based on their microinverters is better, offering scalability and higher efficiency, as currently, 48.90% of EVs in the US are Teslas, and it is three times more common for EV owners to integrate PV systems in their houses than for house owners without an EV, Tesla has a clear customer advantage over Enphase.

But even with its market share being chipped away, as the renewable energy sector is expected to grow at a 10Y CAGR of 15.70%, with solar leading this growth, I still am to an extent bullish.

Looking above, you can see that from the current time to 2050, so through the next 25 years, the renewables sector is expected to grow from about 1 trillion kilowatt-hours, to about 2.4 trillion kilowatt-hours, accounting for 44.00% of the US's generated electricity.

This increase in renewable energy usage is expected to be powered mainly by solar, which is expected to grow from about 19.00% of the total renewable electricity market to 51.00%. Growing from about 0.3 trillion kilowatt-hours in 2024 to about 1.4 trillion kilowatt-hours in 2050.

So in my eyes, even if Tesla were to conquer a large percentage of the market, with it expected to grow this much into the future, I would expect Enphase to secure a decent amount of customers, which would allow for its growth.

9 Pillar Analysis

Nevertheless, now that we have gone through the market overview, let's take a look at the fundamental 9 Pillar Analysis, and see how well the company has been doing through the past few years.

Above you can see that Enphase gets a rating of 6/8, with both pillars in the red being the valuation, so first, let's zoom in on that.

Valuation (Pillars 1 and 2)

Looking at the 5Y PE, it is sitting at 61.69, and has been increasing at a 5Y CAGR of 110.00%. This means that, very steadily, investors have been overpaying more and more for the company's earnings. Currently, the PE is sitting at 67.89. Though something interesting I want to point out is that as 2024 has been quite a bad year for the company, they made only $102.66M in net income, compared to the $438.94M in 2023. If we compare their current price to the 5Y average net income, we would get a PE of 27.03, which looks much better than the 5Y PE of 61.69.

Regarding the P/FCF, currently it is sitting at an attractive value of only 14.45, compared to the 5Y average of 28.27. As the FCF is arguably more important than the net income, as it is what is directly used for share buybacks, innovation, paying down debt, etc, I would actually consider Enphase quite undervalued, that is, if they can retain their financial growth.

Financial Growth (Pillars 3, 4 and 5)

Regarding their revenue, it has been growing at a CAGR of 20.69%, growing from $774.00M in 2020, to $2.29B in 2023, but then falling by 41.95% to $1.33B in 2024.

Regarding the net income, that has grown from $134.00M in 2020, to $438.94M in 2023, to then fall to $102.66M in 2024. All this growth accounts for a 5Y CAGR of 23.12%, which again, similarly to the revenue, is very decent.

Finally, regarding the FCF, that has reached its peak in 2022, reaching a whopping $698.37M, growing by more than 300.00% since 2020, when it sat at only $195.78M. Though after 2022, it started slipping, first falling to $586.38M in 2023, and in 2024, it fell even lower to $480.09M, which though is still much higher than the 2024 net income, which is something I love.

Debt (Pillar 9)

Overall, it does seem like the company has experienced some extreme growth in the past 5 years, but due to the headwinds it has been lately facing, it hasn't been performing too well. Nevertheless, let's take a look at the final pillar, Pillar 9, the debt.

Over the past 5 years, the company has grown their LTL by a lot. They have risen from $57.00M in 2020 to $1.41B in 2024. This, although slightly alarming, is still quite manageable as the company generates an average of $452.08M in FCF YOY. And as Enphase's LTL/5Y FCF - Dividends ratio is sitting at 0.79, it would take them around 4 years to pay off all their LTL, which is a very decent and manageable time frame.

Something I also want to point out is that the company's Current Ratio has been decreasing over the past 5 years, from 1.68 in 2020 to 1.34 in 2024. It is still sitting above one, but is something I will certainly keep my eyes on.

Overall, it does look like that through the past 5 years, the company has been becoming more and more fairly priced, but 2024 was a tough year for them. Their financials have decreased from last year, which as I already mentioned, was mainly caused by competition (mainly from Tesla) and lowering solar panel and equipment prices, which has led to a decrease in profitability. In fact, in 2024, the profit margin sat at only 7.72%, the lowest it has been over the past 5 years, as you can see below.

Now, before moving to the conclusion, I want to calculate the company's intrinsic value, and see if currently, with reasonable EPS and FCF predictions, they would be considered a BUY.

Intrinsic Valuation

First, looking at the Graham's Formula, we will take the 5Y EPS of $1.22 and project a 5Y EPS CAGR of 17.67%. Based on that, the base PE of 7, the growth rate multiplier of 1.5, and the current and average yields on AAA Corp. bonds, we will get an intrinsic value of $36.61, 29.45% lower than the current trading price of $51.89.

For the second valuation model, we will look to the DCF model, where I will take the average FCF from the past 5 years, and based off of that, predict the FFCF using a 10Y + CAGR of 7.70%. I will then discount all of that FFCF using a rate of 8.67% and get an intrinsic value of $70.79.

Finally, we will average out the two values we calculated and get an ultimate intrinsic value of $53.70, 3.49% higher than the current trading price of $51.89. With a margin of safety of 10.00% our acceptable BUY price would be at $48.33, which the company has very recently reached.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I do have to rate Enphase as a BUY. This decision is, in my eyes, perfectly validated by the company's huge growth potential coming from the expected rise in solar power consumption. The rating also takes into account the potential threat from rising competition, such as Tesla. Investing in Enphase certainly carries its risks, but with higher risk comes higher potential reward, and as the company's fundamentals are looking decent, with low debt and high financial growth for most of the past 5 years, for now I retain my BUY rating.

data.title: YMAX Doesn't Deliver Durable Income
data.description:
I have been reviewing YieldMax’s income plans here, here and here. I primarily look for stability of returns and a sweet spot in terms of volatility of the underlying – too high and covered option writing often fails in the longer run, too low and the income generation is not significant. The YieldMax Universe Fund of Option Income ETFs (NYSEARCA:YMAX) looks interesting because it is essentially a fund of funds with equal weighted exposure to YieldMax’s income plans based on selling cash secured calls and spreads on selected stocks. From definition, it looks like ticking the boxes on diversified volatility exposure that hits the sweet spot. I am expecting good and stable returns – let me check.

Different from Other YieldMax Income Funds

Before diving into the nitty-gritty of the fund's performance, some aspects of the fund need to be clarified. This is a fund of fund and does not need any rule-based algorithm, which I found lacking in the case of the funds it holds. The Active Management part of it looks lesser. The fund clearly invests in equal weights in all of its eligible Income plans/ETFs. As of date, the holding report can be downloaded from the YieldMax website. You will see close to equal weighted exposure across 30 underlying YieldMax ETFs as of today – the minor deviations are due to NAV fluctuations of the underlying after entry. Around 1% exposure is in long-term US Government securities and 0.5% is in Cash.

There are two areas of active management in this fund, unlike the other income funds. One, monthly rebalancing to include any new eligible YieldMax income fund launched. Two, in case of extreme losses, the Fund Manager will aim to optimize taxes and redeem the loss positions and replicate the same covered call position as the fund that was redeemed – tax loss harvesting.

Taxability of payouts is a mix of Return of Capital (tax friendly) and Return on Investment, more the latter. Capital gains and taxability is not fully optimized, but that should not be a deterrent if the plan manages to generate super stable high income while preserving capital (akin to the utopian dream)

Management fees for this income fund is the usual 0.99% for all other underlying funds, plus 0.29% Management Fees for the Fund of Fund itself – amounting to 1.28%. This is just a clarification. As we will see, the returns we are talking about – far greater is at stake than the management fee difference. Management fee is perhaps a more pertinent discussion for more conservative and less active funds. YMAX is more than that – it is an income strategy.

The Real Deal - Performance

We will look at three variables here. One being the return distribution characteristics. Secondly, how well this Fund performs in totality, considering returns and capital preservation. Thirdly, evaluate its performance across market conditions.

Since income payout was initially monthly and then changed to weekly, we look at total monthly payout consistency. Consistency may not be a requirement for the return oriented investor – overall quantum probably is a higher priority. However, it remains a priority for those using this ETF purely for monthly income generation.

I was intending to do statistical analysis on this distribution (to satisfy my quant itch), however, not required. The distribution’s consistency is apparent from the graph above. When you receive anywhere between $0.4 to $1 for a month, you know that the search for a stable fixed income plan doesn’t end here. This analysis is more for those investors seeking such consistency in income. Not a fault of the product. Stable income plans would usually mean multi asset strategies primarily based on Fixed Income - option writing is not about that. Option writing income is more about the quantum. We will look at that subsequently.

The second variable - time in the market is acceptable – YMAX has been around for a year. However, its composition has been changing with new products added each month. So, I would wait for the critical performance in April 2025 where the impacts of the market volatility would be more evident. The nearest proxy for a high volatility period was in August 2024 when the VIX crossed 40. However, April 2025 is more likely to be higher and wider – a great case to see how the ETF performs. To remind my readers, I expect option writing strategies to be underperforming by a degree in such periods (almost expecting catastrophic setbacks – imagine income generated on higher premiums on downmoves yes, but either not compensated by the capital loss or the capital recovery not commensurate to opportunity loss of a high volatility rebound). So far, performance, in terms of payout, in Aug 2024 to Sep 24 does not raise concerns (except a potential delayed impact in Sep 2024, impart levels we can live with). We will look at this in more details when comparing overall return behaviors of the fund (capital + income).

Third variable - performance overall. While looking into overall returns, the first thing to note is cumulative payout is decent at 47% if investment was made at inception in December 2023 (over 16 months). This translates to an annualized return of 34%. Note that this is different from the distribution rate mentioned on YieldMax’s website (which is mentioned as an annualized return assuming last dividend payout extrapolated for a year). However, 34% returns as payout in itself is great, provided overall returns including capital changes is reasonable.

Overall investments, analyzed with simplistic assumptions like no tax impact and no reinvestments or time value of received payouts, show a total growth of 10.9% over 16 months (and not including the April 2025 effect). A period where S&P500 returned 20%. That's a story in itself!

Concluding the Case for YMAX

I would close the case for YMAX here on performance grounds alone. But we need to evaluate two more very important sub-theses.

One, how does the entry point impact returns (although an income plan generally should be timing agnostic). Here the impact is significant. This looks more like a plan that needs a good amount of timing to reap overall gains. What makes that difficult is that there is no single underlying (and more new products can be added in future, underlying is not fixed forever) which can be timed for entry. Also, what would you look at to time that entry? Lower underlying volatility or price corrections or momentum? No simple answer emerges to that question. Deeper complicated research can (perhaps) indicate the timing clues, but again that is a one-time exercise – future fund changes will make such research invalid. As of now, no stock specific clue to time entry looks evident. No overall market trends, based on volatility or direction, is seen clearly correlated to the performance data. The performance appears to be tied into the specific structure of the option setup employed by underlying funds, and that is a black box.

On the timing aspect, another additional criteria I look at is the premium or discount to NAV, the fund trades at. I see fluctuations within a tolerable range of 60 bps on both sides – which is lesser than some of the individual YieldMax income plans, but is significant comparing their returns performance.

I had mentioned two sub-theses before writing off YMAX. One, as we saw is the timing aspect of entry. The other aspect is whether we should view the ETF from the performance lens at all. For instance, I could prefer an income plan, however volatile, if it is completed uncorrelated to market conditions and works out with a smoother overall path in the longer term. On that count, enough time under the belt would have helped judge its overall trend and volatility-agnostic performance. So far, we don’t have that length of data. I do not know whether YMAX would fit that bill.

Time for the final call. At the outset, YMAX appealed to me at the concept level. However, its performance leaves room for improvement. Additionally, paths vary by entry timings, which are difficult to control. I do not give Sell ratings on income ETFs. Hence, I will settle for a Hold. I recommend existing investors to shift to underlying specific plans than this Fund of Funds. There too, blind selection of underlying is not advised. As I have noted in my earlier works, income funds based on MSTR (MSTY), Google (GOOY) did not make a strong case, but Bitcoin (YBIT) did. Each fund has to be evaluated separately – I will keep you updated as and when I find better funds like YBIT to invest in.

On that count, enough time under the belt would have helped judge its overall trend volatility-agnostic performance. So far, we don’t have that data. For instance, I could prefer an Income plan, however volatile, if it is completed uncorrelated to market conditions and works out with a smoother overall path in the longer term. I do not know whether YMAX would fit that bill.

Time for the final call. At the outset appealed to me at the concept level, its performance leaves room for improvement. Additionally, paths vary by entry timings, which are difficult to control. I do not give Sell Ratings on Income ETFs. Hence, I will settle for a Hold. However, I am not impressed. I recommend existing investors to shift to underlying specific plans than this Fund of Funds. There too, blind selection of underlying is not advised. As I have noted in my earlier works, Income funds based on Strategy (MSTR), Google (GOOGL)(GOOG) did not make a strong case, but Bitcoin (BTC-USD) did. Each fund has to be evaluated separately – I will keep you updated as and when I find better funds like YBIT to invest in.

data.title: Turning The Tide: Global Markets Outperforming U.S. Equities
data.description:
By Diego Zurita

Since 2000, annual returns of international equities have been better than those of the U.S. only nine times, as shown by Exhibit 1. After the Global Financial Crisis of 2009, the S&P United States LargeMidCap outperformed the S&P World-Ex-U.S. Index in most years.

However, for the first time since 2017, developed market equities have ended the first quarter outperforming the U.S. As concerns grow about the impact of tariffs on the economy and uncertainty looms, market participants appear to be reducing their weight in the U.S. In fact, as illustrated by Exhibit 2, the S&P World Ex-U.S. Index had one of its best first quarters in recent times, while the S&P United States LargeMidCap started in the red. A strong start to the year is not always a definitive predictor of year-end performance, but what might this data suggest about the S&P World Ex-U.S. Index’s current outperformance?

The first quarter of the year was marked by uncertainty caused by geopolitical and economic developments. Tariff threats from the new U.S. administration, talks to end the war in Ukraine, shifting diplomatic relations, political changes in Germany and Canada, and reignited tensions in the Middle East were all part of a challenging beginning of the year. On the monetary front, central banks across the globe may diverge in their decisions around interest rates, but they are expected to proceed with caution due to inflation and recession concerns.

As of the end of March, almost all European countries that constitute the S&P World Index started on a positive note. Germany, the U.K., France and Switzerland have been the main contributors to the outperformance of the S&P World Ex-U.S. Index. In contrast, the S&P United States LargeMidCap was among the worst performers in developed markets, decreasing 4.5%.

Among the GICS® sectors in the S&P World Ex-U.S. Index, which mostly had positive performance, there is one that stands out: Financials. The S&P World Ex-U.S. Financials (Sector) Index rose 12.2% in Q1 2025. This sector has been the main driver behind the outperformance of the S&P World Ex-U.S. Index, contributing to almost half of its increase (46%). This growth may possibly be attributed to positive economic surprises, resilience in European bank earnings and the effects on the economy of a potential end to the war in Ukraine.

Additionally, the Industrials sector, which gained 6.1%, has been an important contributor to the performance of the S&P World Ex-U.S. Index, as defense spending is expected to increase. Utilities and Energy were also among the best-performing sectors of the first quarter, as the European Commission launched a plan that aims to increase investments that contribute to energy security in the region.

Amid geopolitical tensions and evolving trade policies, international equities from developed markets in 2025 have outperformed the U.S. YTD, as some market participants are turning their focus to other geographies. Given the recent economic landscape in the U.S. and around the world, the S&P World Ex-U.S. Index is a relevant benchmark for the current market environment.
